speaker: interviewer
text: Sala, I'd like to take you back and ask you one more question. After your mother passed away and you were alone, how did you cope with that loneliness? What did you do to help yourself get adjusted?

speaker: interviewee
text: To get adjusted? I was very lonely, but I guess, you know, I had to go on. I was busy working. And I was with people that helped. I wasn't all alone. I was with the big crowd of people. So, and all of us had a lot of in common. There was-- you know, everybody was missing somebody. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: So your friendships sustained you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. I was-- got closer to some of the people. And I met a-- a family, a nice Jewish family, really lovely lady. She was evacuated also from-- from Kharkov she was to Tashkent. Because, you know, lots of people had to leave the Russian-- the German occupied Russia some of the cities. So they had to leave also. And she was-- she her family. She had her husband. So she had a much easier life. And she-- I helped her. I used to go there. I washed clothes. I brought water for her. In bucket, I used to carry water to her. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: But you liked to be with people?

speaker: interviewee
text: I liked to be with people. And she felt so sorry for me. I felt like I have a little support. And a couple of times, I was sick. I was in a hospital. They cut off my hair twice. I got sick. And when I was laying in hospital, I said, oh, god. I saw some other sick people. And family was coming to visit them. And I had nobody that came except maybe a friend came. But, you know, I wanted my mom. So it was hard. But I guess a person becomes strong. I don't know. I could never believe that I will live this through and I will survive. Because I was the youngest. I was very attached.

speaker: interviewee
text: In fact, I had an uncle. He used to always tease me. He used to say, what you going to do when you-- when you get married? You won't have your mother with you. And I said, yes, she will be with me, because I'm the youngest.

speaker: interviewee
text: I take it her in to live with me, I said to him. Because I couldn't live without her a day. I was really ashamed. I was a big girl. And I still was attached to her. She had to put me to bed maybe when I was 13 years old. She still has to sit with me, very, very attached. So I don't know how a person could survive everything. But I guess that's human nature or something that you become strong. You suffer, but you still fight.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a big trauma for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was. And sometimes when I came to this country, for many years, I suffered from my stomach. I was to go to doc, even in Germany, right away. And I used to go to the doctors and the hospitals just to check me. They couldn't find nothing. And I was sick. I couldn't eat. I was feeling nausea-- nerves. I suffered a lot from nerves from the depression, a lot. Even when the kids were small, I was always sick with my stomach or with my crying. And now, I became a little stronger, a little bit.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you still need people with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I need people. [SOBBING] I can't be alone. I get-- and I have to keep busy. I love to keep busy. I cannot just relax. I guess that goes back, yeah? I can relax only in the evening. If there's supper, I like to watch television and read in the morning. When it's still early and everybody's sleeping, I take a walk. But during the day, I just can't-- you know, I have to-- I'm jittery.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you went back to Sosnowiec and you went to speak to some of the Polish neighbors, your old neighbors--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --what was your overwhelming feeling when you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: They just asked me what happened to my parents. And they advised me. They told me that there is another neighbor of ours that used to live next door to us that their girls survived the war and that she is-- they told me which hotel she is staying.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went to see this neighbor of mine. But we didn't talk about anti-Semitism or about the Germans with them. I just ask if there is some-- they ask me who survived, you know, from our family.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was there with them for an hour. And they-- they told me where I should find this neighbor, a Jewish girl also that survived. So I went to see her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they kind to you when you came back?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Pole? The Pole? They didn't bother me. It's just that on the-- on the way going to our house, I saw some boys that we grew up in the same time about the same age. And they told me, look, you were a little girl. Look, now, you are a woman. And I was kind of a little bit heavy when I came after the war. I started eating, you know, in Germany, peanut butter. And they gave us some rations, peanut butter. And I was hungry, so I started eating a lot of bread. I got-- they told me. They made a remark. Look how-- because I was always very small and tiny. Look how heavy. What kind of a-- a woman, a big woman and you are. But they didn't ask too many question, who survived or what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you decide to come to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- when I was single still in Germany-- in Germany-- I had plans to go to Israel to a kibbutz, or whatever, 1948. But when I met my husband, he said he had different plans. He wanted to come to the United States. Because there were a lot of bad letters coming from Israel. There was no housing, no. And somehow he wanted to come have a little bit easier. And that's why he came to the States.

speaker: interviewer
text: When your boat came into New York harbor, your ship, what was the first thing that you saw?

speaker: interviewee
text: What was the first thing what I saw? We came in this-- this big-- what's the name of that? Eyes-- island?

speaker: interviewer
text: Ellis Island?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ellis Island. And the people were very-- there was from the Jewish joint, the people were very friendly. They gave us donuts. And-- and they put us in a train to go to Louisville, Kentucky. But when I came to Louisville, I was really depressed. I cried. I had nobody. And, you know, it wasn't this what I expected. if-- I couldn't speak. And like I told you before, I came into our room-- rooming house.

speaker: interviewee
text: The woman took me around. And she said, you know how to put light on? And it was so depressing, so dark in this house. And I was very disappointed.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was-- I was-- so I moved out from this house. And I moved to a different rooming house. And there was a gentle lady. And she was so nice to me. She took me to the stores. She explained to me, because I couldn't speak English. So she went shopping with me. And she explained to me what this is, what this is. And I felt more comfortable in this house. And then my husband got a job. He started working. And I got pregnant. We wanted to start a family.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was excited with the baby. And I didn't work, because I got pregnant right away. I didn't work. My husband went to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: And six months later, being in Kentucky, I receive a letter from my sister that she's coming to Milwaukee working. Because her husband, home, he had a little store with beer and with soda. They usually put-- put you where you can get a job.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my brother-in-law was working at Bliss-- Schlitz company. So my sister came to Milwaukee with her son. And her daughter was already married.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were in Munich, they were. And he was studying to become a doctor, her son-in-law. And she was a dentist. She was going to be a dentist my niece.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she came just with her son. And her daughter was still in Germany. And we wanted to be together. So I was pregnant already, my-- maybe seven, eight month. I came to be with my sister in Milwaukee.

speaker: interviewee
text: We took together apartment for a short time till the baby came, then with separate.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that better for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that better for you, when you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, much better that, you know, I was with my sister, with my nephew, sure. Then I had the baby, so. But still I suffered. When I had the baby, too, I-- I was thinking back all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wanting to show your mother the baby?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Missing, missing-- I saw neighbors. And the mother's are coming and the sisters, brothers. And but I was happy to have this one sister. And she was like a mother to me. She was-- she's 19 years older than I am. And my brother-in-law was very nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- did you find that the friends you made in this country were also survivors?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only survivors.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think so?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. We first of all, we-- we went to the same temple. And we're-- and we had a American-- new American club. And we were always together. Barmitzvahs, birthdays, we always stick together.

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't have American friends. Isn't that funny? I don't know why, but that's what happens. They-- you understood each other.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We had so much in common, so much in common till today.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are the names of your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Helen Sectzer and Rochelle Naimon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think that your experiences in the Holocaust affected the way you raised your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think that the children are affected the way I brought them up, yeah. Over-- I was overprotective. And I was always sad. There wasn't a happy atmosphere, always tension. I think it's affected them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do Do you think it affected your marriage?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think that the Holocaust and your experiences affected how you believe in God?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. It did.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way did it affect that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, because sometimes we think if there would be some-- something, why would those little kids and all that peoples be so roughly treated, you know? And you ask questions, but you-- you are brought up in a religious way. So you can't get away from it completely.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, you know, so you try to have your traditional home. And especially my husband, he is more on the religious side than I am. He wants kosher. And that's-- so I try to please him.

speaker: interviewee
text: But my brothers, they are very-- a from that, everything. They don't. They don't even go on Rosh Hashanah for the holidays. And they were brought up in such a religious home. So I don't know if it's because of the war that happened to them. They were-- they were, before the war, they were also more modern. You know, they-- they didn't go anymore in the Jewish clothes. They didn't go to the temples. But now, they're completely away.

speaker: interviewer
text: So do you feel in-- in bad times or times when you're down that you don't call on God for help?

speaker: interviewee
text: I do. I do call on God, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You still--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So somewhere inside of you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It's still--

speaker: interviewer
text: --is still that deep feeling.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I cannot say, you know, there is nothing. But you get confused many times when you think back what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's difficult. Do you think about the Holocaust often? Do the memories come to you at night?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not so much at night, no. But we-- when we get together with friends-- with friends, we always usually come out at the same [INAUDIBLE] But, you know, time heals. I have to admit that time heals, you know? It's just that I am not such a easygoing person like I would like to be. And I am a worrier. I guess that has to do with something the way what I went through. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is-- this is-- I wish I am easy going, can smiling, can-- you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I don't know if I was born with those genes. Or, is that what I went through it? I think so.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes when I talk to the doctors, you know, once I tried to go to a psychiatrist. And because I couldn't sleep nights. And I lost weight. And I was very nervous.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that when my doctor said why don't you go for help, you know. And I went there once, twice. I start talking to a lady that interviewed me. And she said, there is wonder at all that you have those-- Symptoms.

speaker: interviewee
text: --symptoms, yeah, not at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: If If you had a message to leave to your children, your grandchildren, and to the people who are learning and studying from this interview today, what would it be? What-- what would you like to tell people that you've learned from your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well Well, I hope that there would never something like that happen again and that my children should have a better life, a better future, that we-- what we went through. They should never know about something like that. It's just impossible what one person could do to have another. It's unbelievable that something like that can happen. Nobody-- nobody can-- who didn't go through that-- can imagine. Even when you see the pictures on television and on-- they tell me that I wasn't in the concentration camps. But when my husband, my brothers, start telling me the stories, they still cannot prescribe everything that happened. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So would you-- you would like to tell people what?

speaker: interviewee
text: They should-- they should never experience something like that. It should never happen like that again. That people should help each other and love each other instead of such a hatred.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sala, thank you so much for your testimony.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome. You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. And who is in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mother. And this picture was taken 1916 in Szczekociny in this little town where I was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what country was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Poland. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I have this picture from some relatives that sent it to me from Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my father. And this picture was taken probably around 1916 also. And it was sent to me from Israel from my relatives.

speaker: interviewer
text: And And what was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Motl Fromer.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- in Poland, in Szczekociny. This is my sister Rochella and her husband. They were married during the war when I was already not home, when I wasn't home anymore. And this picture probably was taken very shortly before the war, probably in 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Or, '40. His name was Monek Pergricht

speaker: interviewer
text: And did-- did your sister and her husband survive the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They both did not survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is-- I belong to organization, Zionist organization, [INAUDIBLE] Not in Sosnowiec. And this is probably was taken around 1938 or '39, shortly before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where are you located in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the right side, the first one, and end there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which row?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the up-- on and upper row.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the top. Did any of your friends from this group survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: There are few that survived. And some of them are-- are-- I am in touch with them. And I see them once in a while. They live in Canada and New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how were you able to save this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, a-- a friend of-- a girlfriend, one of these girls this is in the picture, she send it to me. She must have had one from home. And she made some copies. And she sent it to the rest of us. This is my brother and his wife. They didn't survive the war also.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your brother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rubin Fromer.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rachel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were this-- was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in Sosnowiec. And at that time, the anti-Semitism started already. And this was the street that Jews were allowed to go. And on the opposite side where the gentiles.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were already restricted as to where you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were restricted. We were-- yeah. The Jews couldn't mix together with the gentiles, were restricted to go on the right side and the gentiles on the left. It was a beautiful street. I can remember like now, restaurants and nice stores.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how were you able to save this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Also, I got it from Israel from someone relatives. This is my husband and me. That picture was taken in Germany when we got engaged. It was in 1948.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your husband's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Aron Mydlak. This is me and Helen-- and my first child Helen. And when she was [SOBBING] born, she was everything to me. My mother, my father, I didn't know. I thought she was an angel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Milwaukee.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: And she was here maybe six, seven months old, maybe nine. I don't remember exactly.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- she was born in 1950. This-- this is my husband and me at Helen's wedding in 19-- in '71. It was a beautiful wedding. We were very excited.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in temple Beth El in Milwaukee. This is myself, my husband Aron, and my two daughters. On the right side is Helen, and the left, Rochelle. And that was done about 1985.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken at my granddaughter's birthday.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Milwaukee?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Milwaukee. Those are my three grandchildren, Jessica. She's 18. She's on the right. And Lisa, in the middle, 21. And Danielle is 14.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was at-- Jessica's graduation in 194-- 19-- 19-- what?

speaker: interviewer
text: In Milwaukee?

speaker: interviewee
text: Milwaukee. Those are my two grandchildren, my younger daughter's kids. [? Allison ?] is here, five. And Jonathon is two. It was taken in 1960--

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Milwaukee it was taken.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is the little boy's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jonathon and Alyssa. I want to introduce my family. This is my husband Aron. And he's holding on his lap my grandson, Jonathon. And I am holding [? Allison. ?] On my left side is my daughter Rochelle and my daughter Helen and my-- my granddaughter Danielle. We're a very close and loving family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Aron, would you like to say something?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Aron Mydlak. I'm holding my grandson, the only grandson that I have in the family. I'm very-- I am very grateful I reach that age. I never think that I'm going to reach that age, because I went through lots of stuff in my life. And thanks God for everything. I have a good two daughters, son-in-laws. And let's hope for the best.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I just want to say that it's an honor and a privilege to have been raised by my parents. They did everything that they possibly could beyond their power to be wonderful, loving, giving parents. And I love them both with all my heart.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Helen. And I knew I would get emotional.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: But how can you not? I have two wonderful caring parents. I know that we were their whole lives. And as a result of that, I feel that my children are everything to me. We are a very close knit family. I couldn't even live as far as away from Chicago, which is an hour and a half drive from here. But they're are wonderful caring parents. And they mean everything to me. And I'm thrilled that they're doing this.

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandparents are very special to me. And I'm glad we're all so close and they're always there for me when I need them. And I love you both very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: I love grandma and grandpa.

speaker: interviewee
text: Me too. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: Very nice. Very nice.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] Give me a kiss. Come here.

speaker: interviewee
text: I wish that they live to be 100 years old.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And may I say that I feel bad that Lisa and Jessica are not here. And they really feel bad about that. Lisa's 21 years old. And Jessica's 18. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: In school.

speaker: interviewee
text: They love their grandparents dearly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you all for your testimony.

speaker: interviewee
text: And thank you very much--

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: --for such sweet people. And we're very grateful that they came and spent some time. It's been very appreciated. I enjoy their company. They've been terrific. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
